preamble: The transformation of an alveolar nasal [n] into a voiced velar plosive [g] is a documented phonetic shift, primarily occurring through processes of assimilation and subsequent denasalization. This involves a change in both the place of articulation, from alveolar to velar, and the manner of articulation, from nasal to plosive. Such shifts often arise in specific phonological environments, particularly when the nasal consonant is adjacent to a velar plosive.
phoneticEffects: Place assimilation (alveolar to velar), denasalization (nasal to plosive), fortition

examples.note: In Prakrit, the Sanskrit sequence of a velar plosive followed by an alveolar nasal ([gn]) underwent assimilation and denasalization, resulting in a geminated velar plosive ([ɡɡ]). The alveolar nasal [n] assimilated to the preceding velar [g], becoming a velar nasal [ŋ], which then denasalized and merged with the following [g] to form [ɡɡ].
This shift is documented within the phonological developments observed in Continental Runic inscriptions.

sources: Findell, P. (2012). Phonological Evidence from the Continental Runic Inscriptions. De Gruyter.
